message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 1/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
Best algorithms/techniques to make audio the most comprehensible (e.g. time-scale modification like WSOLA/PSOLA, intelligibility enhancement, silence/pause compression, spectral and prosody processing), AND how "speed listening" works at playback speeds greater than 16x — the perceptual limits of accelerated speech comprehension, training/adaptation effects, and the signal-processing methods that make extreme speed-ups intelligible.

## Claim under review
"The intelligibility threshold for time-compressed speech in normal-hearing listeners is approximately 11 to 16 syllables per second, setting a perceptual upper bound for accelerated speech comprehension."

**Source:** https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/bf03199702 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The ability to perceive time-compressed speech in normal-hearing listeners has been reported to be approximately 11 to 16 syllables per second (Versfeld and Dreschler 2002)."

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

The ability to perceive time-compressed speech in normal-hearing listeners has been reported to be approximately 11 to 16 syllables per second (Versfeld and Dreschler 2002). This research established important baseline measurements for understanding temporal processing limits in normal-hearing populations.

Studies on the effects of time compression of speech on intelligibility, including Versfeld and Dreschler 2002, have shown flawless performance for moderate compression ratios, but a sharp deterioration in intelligibility for compression ratios above about three. 

The Versfeld and Dreschler 2002 study, published in the Journal of the Acoustical Society of America, investigated the relationship between the intelligibility of time-compressed speech and speech in noise in young and elderly listeners. Additionally, Versfeld & Dreschler (2002) was one of the few studies that used materials produced by more than a single talker.

content.content:
# Article Statement on Time-Compressed Speech Perception

**Exact Quote:**
"The ability to perceive time-compressed speech in normal-hearing listeners has been reported to be approximately 11 to 16 syllables per second"

**Citations Attributed:**
- Versfeld and Dreschler (2002)
- Meng et al. (2019)

**What the Figure Represents:**
This measurement represents a **typical range of measured performance** rather than a strict threshold or upper bound. The authors use it as a comparative benchmark when discussing their own findings, which showed normal-hearing young adults could perceive speech at approximately 12.3–17.0 syllables per second (based on combined data from both experiments). The figure serves as a reference point from prior literature to contextualize their results within existing knowledge about temporal processing capabilities in normal-hearing populations.

Web search results for query: ""11 to 16 syllables per second" time-compressed speech normal-hearing"

Links: [{"title":"The importance of temporal-fine structure to perceive time-compressed speech with and without the restoration of the syllabic rhythm | Scientific Reports","url":"https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-29755-x"},{"title":"Frontiers | Neural frequency-tagging of syllables in naturalistic speech is sensitive to background noise and age-related hearing loss","url":"https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/aging-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnagi.2026.1796527/full"},{"title":"The importance of temporal-fine structure to perceive time-compressed speech with and without the restoration of the syllabic rhythm","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9938863/"},{"title":"The recognition of time-compressed speech as a function of age in listeners with cochlear implants or normal hearing","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9557069/"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study | BMC Neuroscience | Full Text","url":"https://bmcneurosci.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/1471-2202-14-74"},{"title":"Non linear time compression of clear and normal speech at high rates","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.07239"},{"title":"Perceptual Learning of Time-Compressed Speech: More than Rapid Adaptation","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3467231/"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about your query:

The upper limit of young normal-hearing listeners to perceive 50% of time-compressed speech ranges from 11 to 16 syllables per second, though this is also characterized by large inter-subject variability.

This finding appears consistently across multiple peer-reviewed studies. Time compression, in which the speech rate is increased while keeping the temporal fine structure intact, is often used when investigating the upper limit of temporal processing for speech perception.

Additionally, a reduced ability to perceive time-compressed speech—not related to any peripheral hearing deficits—is associated with advancing age and central auditory processing deficits.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

This is a critical finding. The cited DOI (10.3758/bf03199702) is **NOT** the Versfeld and Dreschler 2002 paper. It is:

**"A speech-rate intelligibility threshold for speeded and time-compressed connected speech"** by de Haan, H.J., published in *Perception & Psychophysics* 22, 366–372 (1977).

So there's a source-attribution mismatch. The supporting quote attributes the "11 to 16 syllables per second" figure to "Versfeld and Dreschler 2002," but the cited primary source DOI points to a completely different 1977 paper by de Haan. Let me verify the de Haan 1977 paper's actual content and confirm whether the 11-16 figure traces to Versfeld & Dreschler.

I found relevant information about de Haan's 1977 research on speech-rate intelligibility thresholds. Here's what the search results show:

An automated threshold method was developed for determining the maximum rate of speech understood by individual listeners. The first experiment compared thresholds of two types of rapid speech reportedly different in intelligibility: simple speeded speech and speech compressed by the sampling method.

Thresholds for compressed speech were significantly higher than those for speeded speech, an indication that the threshold at the very least reflects intelligibility. Additionally, threshold values were correlated with traditional comprehension test scores, with correlations being generally low, indicating that the method is not a measure of comprehension.

The research also influenced later work on measuring intelligibility thresholds. The time-compression threshold (TCT) is defined as the speech rate (in syllables per second) for which half of the sentences are correctly perceived.

The complete reference is: de Haan, H. J. (1977). A speech-rate intelligibility threshold for speeded and time-compressed connected speech. Perception & Psychophysics, 22, 366–372.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
I now have enough to assess the claim thoroughly. Let me consolidate the findings.

**Key findings:**

1. **Source-attribution mismatch (the central problem):** The cited primary source DOI `10.3758/bf03199702` is **de Haan, H.J. (1977), "A speech-rate intelligibility threshold for speeded and time-compressed connected speech," Perception & Psychophysics, 22, 366–372** — a 1977 paper, NOT the "Versfeld and Dreschler 2002" paper named in the supporting quote. The supporting quote attributes the figure to Versfeld and Dreschler 2002, but the linked source is a different author and year (off by 25 years). So the claim's stated source does not match the source in its own supporting quote.

2. **The "11 to 16 syllables/sec" figure itself is real and well-supported in the literature** — it appears verbatim in multiple peer-reviewed papers (Nature Scientific Reports 2023, PMC9085996), attributed to Versfeld & Dreschler 2002 (and Meng et al. 2019). So the *number* is not fabricated.

3. **However, the claim mischaracterizes what the figure represents.** Per the secondary sources:
   - It is "the upper limit of young normal-hearing listeners to perceive **50% of** time-compressed speech" — i.e., a 50% intelligibility criterion (the "time-compression threshold," TCT), not the point where comprehension ceases / an absolute "perceptual upper bound."
   - It is described "with large inter-subject variability," not a hard universal ceiling.
   - One measurement source (Versfeld & Dreschler 2002, DOI 10.1121/1.1426376 / PubMed 11831813) is actually a study about young vs. elderly listeners and speech-in-noise correlation — it is the empirical origin of the range, but the range is a measured-performance band, not a strict "perceptual upper bound for accelerated speech comprehension."

The claim overreaches by recasting a "50% intelligibility threshold with large variability" as a definite "perceptual upper bound," and it has a source mismatch (cited DOI ≠ the Versfeld & Dreschler 2002 paper in its quote). The underlying number is real, but the cited primary source is the wrong paper.

This is a refutation: the supporting quote's attribution does not match the cited source, and the claim overstates a 50%-intelligibility/large-variability range as a definitive perceptual upper bound.

input.evidence:
SOURCE MISMATCH: The cited primary DOI 10.3758/bf03199702 is de Haan, H.J. (1977), "A speech-rate intelligibility threshold for speeded and time-compressed connected speech," Perception & Psychophysics 22:366-372 — NOT the "Versfeld and Dreschler 2002" paper named in the supporting quote (off by author and 25 years). The actual Versfeld & Dreschler 2002 paper is in J. Acoust. Soc. Am. (PubMed 11831813, about young vs. elderly listeners and speech-in-noise). So the claim's stated source does not match the source attributed inside its own quote.

OVERREACH ON MEANING: The "11 to 16 syllables/sec" number IS real and reproduced verbatim in peer-reviewed sources (Nature Sci Reports s41598-023-29755-x; PMC9085996), but those sources define it as "the upper limit of young normal-hearing listeners to perceive 50% of time-compressed speech" — a 50%-intelligibility threshold (the time-compression threshold, TCT = speech rate at which half the sentences are correctly perceived), explicitly "characterized by large inter-subject variability." The claim recasts this 50%-correct/high-variability band as a definitive "perceptual upper bound for accelerated speech comprehension," which is stronger and more absolute than the literature supports.

VERDICT: The underlying figure is genuine, but the claim (a) cites the wrong primary paper (DOI points to de Haan 1977, quote credits Versfeld & Dreschler 2002), and (b) overstates a variable 50%-intelligibility threshold as a hard perceptual ceiling.
input.counterSource: https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/bf03199702 (cited DOI resolves to de Haan 1977, not Versfeld & Dreschler 2002); https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-29755-x and https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9085996/ (define 11-16 syll/sec as the limit to perceive 50% of time-compressed speech with large inter-subject variability); https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11831813/ (actual Versfeld & Dreschler 2002 paper, JASA)

content.text:
I've completed the verification and submitted my structured output with a refutation verdict.

**Summary:** The claim is refuted on two grounds:

1. **Source mismatch:** The cited primary DOI (10.3758/bf03199702) resolves to de Haan, H.J. (1977), not the "Versfeld and Dreschler 2002" paper named in its own supporting quote. The actual Versfeld & Dreschler 2002 paper is a different publication (JASA, PubMed 11831813).

2. **Overreach:** While the "11 to 16 syllables/sec" figure is genuine and reproduced in peer-reviewed literature, those sources define it as the limit to perceive **50% of** time-compressed speech (a 50%-intelligibility threshold) with **large inter-subject variability** — not the definitive "perceptual upper bound" the claim asserts.

The number is real, but the cited source is the wrong paper and the claim overstates a variable 50%-intelligibility threshold as a hard perceptual ceiling.
